title: Objective of this document
source_quote: Objective of this document

title: Objective of this document
summary: The document provides an accounting of factors and rationale for the Allowable Annual Cut (AAC) determination for the 100 Mile House TSA under Section 8 of the Forest Act, and identifies where new information is needed.
source_quote: This document provides an accounting of the factors I have considered and the rationale I have employed in making my determination, under Section 8 of the Forest Act, of the allowable annual cut (AAC) for the 100 Mile House Timber Supply Area (TSA). This document also identifies where new or better information is needed for incorporation in future determinations.

title: Acknowledgement
source_quote: Acknowledgement

title: Acknowledgement
summary: The author acknowledges contributions from BC Ministry of Forests staff, local residents, First Nations, forestry consultants, and licensees.
source_quote: For preparation of the information I have considered in this determination, I am indebted to staff of the BC Ministry of Forests, Lands and Natural Resource Operations (FLNR)... I am also grateful to the local residents, First Nations, forestry consultants and licensees who contributed to this process.

title: Statutory framework
source_quote: Statutory framework

title: Statutory framework
summary: Section 8 of the Forest Act requires the chief forester to consider specified factors for AAC determination. The deputy chief forester is authorized to carry out these functions under Section 23(3) of the Interpretation Act.
source_quote: Section 8 of the Forest Act requires the chief forester to consider a number of specified factors in determining AACs for timber supply areas (TSAs) and tree farm licences. In addition to the chief forester, Section 23 (3) of the Interpretation Act expressly authorizes the deputy chief forester to carry out the functions of the chief forester... Section 8 of the Forest Act is reproduced in full as Appendix 1 of this document.

title: Section 8 of the Forest Act
summary: Reference to the legal requirement for AAC determination factors.
source_quote: Section 8 of the Forest Act requires the chief forester to consider a number of specified factors in determining AACs for timber supply areas (TSAs) and tree farm licences.

title: Section 23(3) of the Interpretation Act
summary: Authorization for deputy chief forester to act.
source_quote: Section 23 (3) of the Interpretation Act expressly authorizes the deputy chief forester to carry out the functions of the chief forester, including those required under Section 8 of the Forest Act.

section_path: Description of the 100 Mile House TSA
title: Description of the 100 Mile House TSA
source_quote: Description of the 100 Mile House TSA

section_path: Description of the 100 Mile House TSA
title: Description of the 100 Mile House TSA
summary: The 100 Mile House TSA is in south-central BC, bounded by the Fraser River, Cariboo Mountains, Williams Lake TSA, and Kamloops TSA. It covers 1.24 million hectares, with 670,000 hectares available for timber harvesting. The area features varied topography and climate, with dominant species like lodgepole pine and Douglas-fir, and habitat for wildlife including species at risk.
source_quote: The 100 Mile House TSA is located in south-central British Columbia... The total area of the 100 Mile House TSA is about 1.24 million hectares... After all other resource requirements have been accounted for, about 670 000 hectares are considered available for timber harvesting... The dominant tree species in the TSA are lodgepole pine and Douglas-fir... Species at risk in the TSA include: mountain caribou, grizzly bear, bighorn sheep, and the prairie falcon.

section_path: Description of the 100 Mile House TSA
title: Total TSA Area
summary: Total area of the 100 Mile House TSA.
source_quote: The total area of the 100 Mile House TSA is about 1.24 million hectares

section_path: Description of the 100 Mile House TSA
title: Forested Crown Land Area
summary: Area of forested Crown land, parks, and protected areas.
source_quote: about 840 000 hectares are forested Crown land, parks and protected areas

section_path: Description of the 100 Mile House TSA
title: Available Harvesting Land Base
summary: Area considered available for timber harvesting after other resource requirements.
source_quote: about 670 000 hectares are considered available for timber harvesting

title: Rationale for 100 Mile House TSA - Introduction and Context
source_quote: AAC Rationale for 100 Mile House TSA, November 2013

title: TSA Description, Communities, and Aboriginal History
summary: The 100 Mile House TSA features diverse forests providing timber, forage, and habitat. It supports outdoor recreation and includes major communities like 100 Mile House and Clinton, with a combined population of approximately 23,000. The area has significant First Nations presence, including three bands physically located within the TSA and many others with overlapping traditional territories.
source_quote: The forests of the 100 Mile House TSA are very diverse... There is a rich, diverse aboriginal history in the 100 Mile House TSA.

title: Combined Population
summary: The combined population within and surrounding the TSA communities is approximately 23,000.
source_quote: The combined population within and surrounding these communities is approximately 23,000.

title: Economic Dependency
summary: Based on 2006 data, the forest sector accounts for 26% of total basic employment in the district. Other key sectors include public sector (26%), tourism (16%), construction (16%), agriculture (8%), and mining (2%).
source_quote: Based on the report entitled Economic Dependency Tables for Forest Districts (2006), the forest sector accounts for 26 percent of total basic employment...

title: History of the AAC
source_quote: History of the AAC

title: AAC Historical Values
summary: The AAC was 1,250,000 m3 (1981-1995), 1,362,000 m3 (1996-2001), and 1,334,000 m3 (2002). In 2006, it was increased to 2.0 million m3 due to the mountain pine beetle epidemic, with 90% of harvest volume expected to come from stands with 70% or more pine.
source_quote: From 1981 to 1995, the AAC for the 100 Mile House TSA was 1 250 000 cubic metres... In 2006... the chief forester increased the AAC to 2.0 million cubic metres.

title: Harvest Volume Composition Assumption
summary: The Chief Forester expected 90 percent of the total harvest volume to originate from stands with 70 percent or more pine.
source_quote: The chief forester indicated that it was his expectation that 90 percent of the total harvest volume originate from stands with 70 percent or more pine.

title: Apportionment of the AAC
source_quote: Table 1 shows the apportionment of the AAC by the Minister of Forests, Lands and Natural Resource Operations, effective March 14, 2007.

title: Table 1. Apportionment of the AAC
summary: Table detailing the apportionment of the 2,000,000 m3 AAC as of March 14, 2007. Categories include Forest Licences Replaceable (802,782 m3), Non-Replaceable (513,897 m3), BCTS (456,071 m3), Pulpwood Agreement (112,000 m3), Community Forest (20,000 m3), Woodlot (20,000 m3), FSR (5,250 m3), and Small Scale Salvage (70,000 m3).
source_quote: Table 1. Apportionment of the AAC... Forest Licences Replaceable 802 782... Total 2 000 000

title: New AAC determination
source_quote: New AAC determination

title: AAC Phasing and Constraints
summary: Effective Nov 7, 2013, the AAC is 2,000,000 m3 until Nov 7, 2018 (with max 500,000 m3 from live trees). From Nov 7, 2018 onwards, it drops to 1,000,000 m3 (with max 500,000 m3 from live trees). A new determination is required within 10 years but may be revisited in 5 years based on monitoring.
source_quote: Effective November 7, 2013, the new AAC for the 100 Mile House TSA will be as follows...

title: Live Tree Harvest Limit
summary: No more than 500,000 cubic metres of the AAC are attributable to live trees for both the 2013-2018 and post-2018 periods.
source_quote: of which no more than 500 000 cubic metres are attributable to live trees.

title: Guiding principles for AAC determinations
summary: Section heading introducing the guiding principles for AAC determinations.
source_quote: Guiding principles for AAC determinations

title: Overview of Guiding Principles
summary: Establishes the need for consistency in AAC determinations under Section 8 of the Forest Act, outlining the joint establishment of guiding principles by the chief forester and deputy chief foresters, while reserving the right to deviate with explanation.
source_quote: Section 8 of the Forest Act requires the chief forester to consider particular factors in determining the AACs for timber supply areas and tree farm licences. ... we, the chief forester and deputy chief foresters, jointly established the following body of guiding principles.

title: Forest Act Section 8
summary: Reference to the legal requirement under Section 8 of the Forest Act for considering specific factors in AAC determinations.
source_quote: Section 8 of the Forest Act requires the chief forester to consider particular factors in determining the AACs for timber supply areas and tree farm licences.

title: Ministry of Forests and Range Act Section 4
summary: Reference to the mandate of the Ministry of Forests, Lands and Natural Resource Operations as set out in Section 4 of the Ministry of Forests and Range Act.
source_quote: the mandate of the Ministry of Forests, Lands and Natural Resource Operations as set out in Section 4 of the Ministry of Forests and Range Act

title: Forest and Range Practices Act (FRPA)
summary: Reference to responsibilities under the Forest Act and Forest and Range Practices Act.
source_quote: our responsibilities under the Forest Act and Forest and Range Practices Act (FRPA)

section_path: Guiding principles for AAC determinations > Integrated decision making
title: Integrated decision making
summary: Section heading regarding the integrated approach to resource management.
source_quote: Integrated decision making

section_path: Guiding principles for AAC determinations > Integrated decision making
title: Objective of Integrated Approach
summary: The Ministry aims to take an integrated approach to all resource management decisions, considering all resource values and the interactions of management on timber supply.
source_quote: One of the key objectives of the Ministry of Forests, Lands and Natural Resource Operations is to take an integrated approach to all resource management decisions that considers all resource values.

section_path: Guiding principles for AAC determinations > Information uncertainty
title: Information uncertainty
summary: Section heading addressing uncertainty in AAC determinations.
source_quote: Information uncertainty

section_path: Guiding principles for AAC determinations > Information uncertainty
title: Managing Uncertainty in AACs
summary: Discusses methods for dealing with uncertainty, including managing risks through evaluation and frequent re-determination. It asserts that decisions should not be based on speculative practices or unfinalized land-use decisions, though designated protected areas are deducted from the harvestable land base.
source_quote: Two important ways of dealing with this uncertainty are: (i) managing risks by evaluating the significance of specific uncertainties... and (ii) re-determining AACs frequently... It is not appropriate to speculate on timber supply impacts that may eventually result from land-use decisions not yet finalized by government.

section_path: Guiding principles for AAC determinations > Information uncertainty
title: Basis for Decisions
summary: Decisions must not be based on proposed or potential practices not substantiated by demonstrated performance or beyond current legal requirements.
source_quote: It is not appropriate to base decisions on proposed or potential practices that could affect the timber supply but are not substantiated by demonstrated performance or are beyond current legal requirements.

section_path: Guiding principles for AAC determinations > Climate change
title: Climate change
summary: Section heading regarding climate change considerations.
source_quote: Climate change

section_path: Guiding principles for AAC determinations > Climate change
title: Climate Change and AACs
summary: Acknowledges scientific agreement on climate change and its effects on forests, despite uncertainty in specific characteristics. It states that findings will be considered as research evolves and that vulnerability assessments are useful. It distinguishes between responding to uncertain future impacts and known processes like MPB infestation.
source_quote: One key area of uncertainty relates to climate change. ... there is substantial scientific agreement that climate is changing, that the changes will affect forest ecosystems, and that forest management practices will need to be adapted.

section_path: Guiding principles for AAC determinations > First Nations
title: First Nations
summary: Section heading regarding First Nations consultation.
source_quote: First Nations

section_path: AAC Rationale for 100 Mile House TSA, November 2013
title: AAC Rationale for 100 Mile House TSA, November 2013
summary: Start of the rationale document discussing base case assumptions.
source_quote: AAC Rationale for 100 Mile House TSA, November 2013

section_path: Base Case Assumptions and Harvest Levels
title: Base Case Assumptions and Harvest Levels
summary: The base case models accelerated harvesting to salvage dead timber. It assumes dead pine retains value for 15 years. Initial harvest level is 2.0 M m3/yr for 7 years, then 890,000 m3/yr for 48 years, before rising to 1.6 M m3/yr.
source_quote: The base case modelled the current practice of accelerated harvesting... Starting in 2012, the initial harvest level in the 150-year base case was set at 2.0 million cubic metres per year.

section_path: Base Case Assumptions and Harvest Levels
title: Dead Pine Commercial Value Duration
summary: It is assumed that dead pine trees retain commercial value until fifteen years after being killed by MPB.
source_quote: For the base case, it was assumed that dead pine trees retain commercial value until fifteen years after being killed by MPB.

section_path: Base Case Assumptions and Harvest Levels
title: Initial Harvest Level
summary: The initial harvest level was set at 2.0 million cubic metres per year, maintainable for seven years.
source_quote: Starting in 2012, the initial harvest level in the 150-year base case was set at 2.0 million cubic metres per year. This rate... can be maintained for seven years.

section_path: Base Case Assumptions and Harvest Levels
title: Mid-term Harvest Level
summary: After 2019, the harvest level decreases to 890,000 cubic metres per year and remains at this level for 48 years.
source_quote: After 2019, the harvest level decreases to 890 000 cubic metres per year and remains at this level for 48 years (2067).

section_path: Base Case Assumptions and Harvest Levels
title: Long-term Sustainable Level
summary: The harvest level increases to a long-term sustainable level of 1.6 million cubic metres per year in 2086.
source_quote: after which it begins to increase to the long-term sustainable level of 1.6 million cubic metres per year in 2086.

section_path: Consideration of factors as required by Section 8 (8) of the Forest Act
title: Consideration of factors as required by Section 8 (8) of the Forest Act
summary: Review of factors required by the Forest Act for determining Allowable Annual Cut.
source_quote: Consideration of factors as required by Section 8 (8) of the Forest Act

section_path: Consideration of factors as required by Section 8 (8) of the Forest Act
title: Factors Accepted as Modelled
summary: Factors accepted as modeled are listed in Table 2. Factors with little uncertainty or where modeling appropriately represents current management are not discussed further.
source_quote: I have reviewed the information for all of the factors required to be considered under Section 8 of the Forest Act... These factors are listed in Table 2.

section_path: Consideration of factors as required by Section 8 (8) of the Forest Act
title: Table 2. List of factors accepted as modelled
summary: Table listing Forest Act subsections and corresponding factors accepted as modelled in the base case, such as land classification, growth rates, and silvicultural treatments.
source_quote: Table 2. List of factors accepted as modelled

section_path: Section 8 (8) In determining an allowable annual cut...
title: Section 8 (8) In determining an allowable annual cut
summary: Legal text regarding the consideration of timber production rates and forest composition.
source_quote: Section 8 (8) In determining an allowable annual cut under subsection (1) the chief forester... must consider (a) the rate of timber production that may be sustained on the area... (i) the composition of the forest and its expected rate of growth on the area

section_path: Land base contributing to timber harvesting
title: Land base contributing to timber harvesting
summary: Discussion of the timber harvesting land base (THLB) calculations and deductions.
source_quote: Land base contributing to timber harvesting

section_path: Land base contributing to timber harvesting
title: Total TSA Area
summary: The total area of the 100 Mile House TSA is 1,237,626 hectares.
source_quote: The total area of the 100 Mile House TSA... is 1 237 626 hectares

section_path: Land base contributing to timber harvesting
title: Forested Crown Land Area
summary: 839,789 hectares are forested Crown land, parks and protected areas.
source_quote: 839 789 hectares are forested Crown land, parks and protected areas.

title: Deciduous Contribution
summary: Section discussing the classification and contribution of deciduous stands to timber supply.
source_quote: District staff maintain that a significant amount of the area classified as deciduous-leading in the inventory is likely mixed conifer and deciduous stands.

title: Deciduous Contribution Summary
summary: The author accepts the inclusion of aspen and birch in the THLB but requests better inventory data to understand deciduous contribution given potential classification inaccuracies.
source_quote: I accept the inclusion of aspen- and birch-leading stands in the THLB and the exclusion of juniper-, white-bark pine- and cottonwood-leading stands from the THLB.

title: Old Growth Management Areas (OGMA)
summary: Section describing OGMAs and their treatment in the timber supply analysis.
source_quote: Old growth management areas (OGMA) were established for the TSA under the Cariboo-Chilcotin Land Use Plan Order.

title: OGMA Exclusion Ratio
summary: The base case assumed 10 percent of permanent and rotational OGMAs may have salvage harvesting, including 7859 hectares in the THLB.
source_quote: The base case assumed that up to 10 percent of permanent and rotational OGMAs may have salvage harvesting and; therefore, included 7859 hectares of OGMAs in the THLB.

title: OGMA Impact on THLB
summary: The inclusion of OGMA salvage areas represents approximately one percent of the THLB.
source_quote: Net of other factors, this inclusion represents approximately one percent of the THLB.

title: OGMA Decision Rationale
summary: The author concludes that including 10 percent of OGMAs results in a one percent overestimation of short-term timber supply and will account for this in the decision.
source_quote: I agree with staff and conclude that the inclusion of 10 percent of permanent and rotational OGMAs in the THLB results in a one percent overestimation in the base case short-term timber supply and I will account for this in ‘Reasons for Decision’.

title: Riparian Areas
summary: Section detailing the lack of comprehensive mapping and the assumptions used for riparian reserves.
source_quote: Comprehensive riparian classification mapping is not available for the 100 Mile House TSA.

title: Riparian Reserve Reduction
summary: The base case used estimated reductions of 1.3 percent for riparian reserves and 0.7 percent for lakeshore management zones.
source_quote: the estimated reductions of 1.3 percent for riparian reserves and 0.7 percent for lakeshore management zones used in the 2001 analysis were used in the base case.

title: Riparian Decision Rationale
summary: The author accepts the current assumptions as best available but requests improved mapping and monitoring before the next determination.
source_quote: I accept that the riparian reserve and lakeshore management zone assumptions used in the base case reflect the best available information and are adequate for use in my determination.

title: Forest Stewardship Retention Plan
summary: Reference to the Tsq’escnem’c Canim Lake Band Forest Stewardship Retention Plan for Snine Forest.
source_quote: The Tsq’escnem’c Canim Lake Band Forest Stewardship Retention Plan for Snine Forest developed by the Canim Lake Indian Band(CLIB) includes ways to mitigate adverse impacts on these values.

title: Forest and Range Practices Act
summary: Reference to legal requirements for timber harvesting.
source_quote: licensees are able to harvest timber provided the applicable legal requirements (e.g., Forest and Range Practices Act) are met.

section_path: Expected Rate of Growth - Natural Stand Yields
title: Expected Rate of Growth - Natural Stand Yields
summary: Section addressing concerns about underestimation of natural stand yields.
source_quote: Yield curves for natural stands were generated using the Variable Density Yield Prediction model version 7 (VDYP7)

section_path: Expected Rate of Growth - Natural Stand Yields
title: Natural Stand Yield Sensitivity
summary: A 10 percent increase in natural stand yields resulted in a 11 percent increase in base case harvest levels.
source_quote: Based on my review of a sensitivity analysis, in which a 10 percent increase in all natural stand yields resulted in a 11 percent increase in the base case harvest levels

section_path: Section 8 (8) (a) (iv) the standard of timber utilization and the allowance for decay, waste and breakage expected to be applied with respect to timber harvesting on the area
title: Section 8 (8) (a) (iv) the standard of timber utilization and the allowance for decay, waste and breakage expected to be applied with respect to timber harvesting on the area
source_quote: Section 8 (8) (a) (iv) the standard of timber utilization and the allowance for decay, waste and breakage expected to be applied with respect to timber harvesting on the area:

section_path: Section 8 (8) (a) (iv) the standard of timber utilization and the allowance for decay, waste and breakage expected to be applied with respect to timber harvesting on the area
title: Utilization standards, dead potential volume, and grade 4 credits
summary: The section reviews log grade adjustments implemented in April 2006, noting that dead potential volume is now charged to cut control. It addresses the underestimation of base case supply due to this change and discusses the impact of grade 4 credits, which allowed harvest rates exceeding the AAC between 2007 and 2011. The chief forester accepts current utilization standards but requests monitoring of waste administration and grade 4 credit extensions.
source_quote: Utilization - log grade adjustments... Since grade 4 includes both live and dead timber, grade 4 credits could increase the amount of live timber being harvested...

title: Dead potential volume estimate
summary: District staff found that 7.2 percent of volume charged was removed from cut control due to species adjustment factors, representing about five percent of the total AAC for the TSA.
source_quote: Based on a review of cut control information, staff found that 7.2 percent of the volume charged has been removed from the cut control of replaceable forest licences due to application of species adjustment factors.  This represents about five percent of the total AAC for the TSA.

title: Base case supply underestimation
summary: The chief forester concludes that the base case short- and mid-term timber supply are underestimated by about five percent because they did not account for dead potential volume now charged to cut control.
source_quote: Since the inventory information used in the base case did not account for the contribution of dead potential volume that is now charged to cut control, I conclude that the base case short- and mid-term timber supply are underestimated by about five percent.

title: Grade 4 credits volume
summary: From January 1, 2007 to December 31, 2011, 1,052,927 cubic metres, or about 16 percent of the volume harvested, were removed from cut control as grade 4 credits.
source_quote: From January 1, 2007 to December 31, 2011, 1 052 927 cubic metres - about 16 percent of the volume harvested over that period - were removed from cut control as grade 4 credits.

title: Cut Control Regulation Section 17(6)
summary: Section 17(6) of the Cut Control Regulation allows licensees to apply for a cut control credit for grade 4 logs shipped to facilities other than sawmills or veneer plants, with the provision expiring June 1, 2014.
source_quote: Section 17 (6) of the Cut Control Regulation allows licensees to apply for a cut control credit for any grade 4 log shipped to a facility other than a sawmill or veneer plant.

section_path: Section 8 (8) (a) (v) the constraints on the amount of timber produced from the area that reasonably can be expected by use of the area for purposes other than timber production
title: Section 8 (8) (a) (v) the constraints on the amount of timber produced from the area that reasonably can be expected by use of the area for purposes other than timber production
source_quote: Section 8 (8) (a) (v) the constraints on the amount of timber produced from the area that reasonably can be expected by use of the area for purposes other than timber production:

title: Integrated resource management, cultural heritage, and moose
summary: The section details IRM objectives guided by the Cariboo-Chilcotin Land Use Plan and other orders. It discusses cultural heritage resources, noting that while some sites may be inappropriately included in the THLB, the base case assumptions are deemed adequate due to small site sizes and operational-level consultations. It also addresses moose habitat concerns, accepting base case assumptions while noting new risk assessment tools being implemented.
source_quote: Integrated resource management - general comments... cultural heritage resources... - moose

title: Ministry of Forests and Range Act
summary: The Ministry is required to manage, protect, and conserve forest and range resources so that timber production and other natural resource values are coordinated and integrated.
source_quote: The Ministry of Forests and Range (now part of the Ministry of Forests, Lands and Natural Resource Operations) is required under the Ministry of Forests and Range Act to manage, protect and conserve the forest and range resources of the Crown...

title: Archaeological site density
summary: One site at the southern end of the TSA contains over 340 culturally-significant depressions over a two-kilometre square area.
source_quote: ...with the exception of one site at the southern end of the TSA where over 340 culturally-significant depressions (e.g., pre-contact habitation pits, roasting pits, human burial remains, etc.) are found over a two-kilometre square area.

title: Adequacy of base case for cultural heritage
summary: The chief forester concludes that base case assumptions for cultural heritage are adequate because sites are small and overlap with other reserves, though acknowledged some identified sites may have been inappropriately included in the THLB.
source_quote: I conclude that it is likely some identified culturally significant sites were inappropriately included in the THLB used in the base case.  However, given the available information, the generally small size of cultural heritage sites and the significant overlap between these sites and areas excluded from timber harvesting to account for other values, I conclude that the assumptions used in the base case are adequate for use in this determination.
